<script type="text/javascript">
var maindomain_lang = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
var main_site_url = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
var ajax_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php';	
function getParameterByName(name, url) {
		if(url == null){
		  url = window.location.href;
		}
	    name = name.replace(/[\[\]]/g, '\\$&');
	    var regex = new RegExp('[?&]' + name + '(=([^&#]*)|&|#|$)'),
		results = regex.exec(url);
	    if (!results) return null;
	    if (!results[2]) return '';
	    return decodeURIComponent(results[2].replace(/\+/g, ' '));
	}
	var container_q = getParameterByName('container');
	var d_lang = 'en';	
</script>

		#wpdevart_info_counter_of_imgs{
			    display: inline-block;
				padding-left:15px;
				padding-right:4px;
				font-size:20px;
				color:#000000;
		}
		#wpdevart_info_caption{
			    display: inline-block;
				padding-left:15px;
				padding-right:4px;
				font-size:20px;
				color:#000000;
		}
		#wpdevart_info_title{
			    display: inline-block;
				padding-left:5px;
				padding-right:5px;
				font-size:15px;
				color:#000000;
		}
		@-webkit-keyframes rotate {
			to   {-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);}
			from  {-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);}
		}
		@keyframes rotate {
			to   {transform: rotate(360deg);}
			from  {transform: rotate(0deg);}
		}
		#wpdevart_lb_loading_img,#wpdevart_lb_loading_img_first{
			-webkit-animation: rotate 2s linear  infinite;
    		animation: rotate 2s linear infinite;
		}
	  </style>      <link rel="icon" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/icon-Unit42-180x180-1.png" sizes="32x32" />
<link rel="icon" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/icon-Unit42-180x180-1.png" sizes="192x192" />

<script>var $ = jQuery;</script>

    <script type="text/javascript">
    ;(function(win, doc, style, timeout) {
    var STYLE_ID = 'at-body-style';
    function getParent() {
    return doc.getElementsByTagName('head')[0];
    }
    function addStyle(parent, id, def) {
        if (!parent) {
        return;
        }
        var style = doc.createElement('style');
        style.id = id;
        style.innerHTML = def;
        parent.appendChild(style);
    }
    function removeStyle(parent, id) {
        if (!parent) {
            return;
        }
        var style = doc.getElementById(id);
        if (!style) {
            return;
        }
        parent.removeChild(style);
    }
    addStyle(getParent(), STYLE_ID, style);
    setTimeout(function() {
    removeStyle(getParent(), STYLE_ID);
    }, timeout);
    }(window, document, "body {visibility:hidden !important}", 3000));
    </script>

            <script src="https://assets.adobedtm.com/9273d4aedcd2/0d76ae0322d7/launch-425c423d843b.min.js" async></script>
            <script type="text/javascript" src="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/content/dam/pan/en_US/includes/attribution.js"></script>


<script type="text/javascript">
var isIE11 = !!navigator.userAgent.match(/Trident.*rv\:11\./);
if(isIE11){
    var polyfill = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/scripts/polyfill.min.js';
    document.write('<script type="text/javascript" src="'+polyfill+'">\x3C/script>');
}
    /**
 * String.prototype.replaceAll() polyfill
 * https://gomakethings.com/how-to-replace-a-section-of-a-string-with-another-one-with-vanilla-js/
 * @author Chris Ferdinandi
 * @license MIT
 */
if (!String.prototype.replaceAll) {
	String.prototype.replaceAll = function(str, newStr){
		// If a regex pattern
		if (Object.prototype.toString.call(str).toLowerCase() === '[object regexp]') {
			return this.replace(str, newStr);
		}
		// If a string
		return this.replace(new RegExp(str, 'g'), newStr);
	};
}

    /*! lozad.js - v1.16.0 - 2020-09-06 */
!function(t,e){"object"==typeof exports&&"undefined"!=typeof module?module.exports=e():"function"==typeof define&&define.amd?define(e):t.lozad=e()}(this,function(){"use strict";
/**
   * Detect IE browser
   * @const {boolean}
   * @private
   */var g="undefined"!=typeof document&&document.documentMode,f={rootMargin:"0px",threshold:0,load:function(t){if("picture"===t.nodeName.toLowerCase()){var e=t.querySelector("img"),r=!1;null===e&&(e=document.createElement("img"),r=!0),g&&t.getAttribute("data-iesrc")&&(e.src=t.getAttribute("data-iesrc")),t.getAttribute("data-alt")&&(e.alt=t.getAttribute("data-alt")),r&&t.append(e)}if("video"===t.nodeName.toLowerCase()&&!t.getAttribute("data-src")&&t.children){for(var a=t.children,o=void 0,i=0;i<=a.length-1;i++)(o=a[i].getAttribute("data-src"))&&(a[i].src=o);t.load()}t.getAttribute("data-poster")&&(t.poster=t.getAttribute("data-poster")),t.getAttribute("data-src")&&(t.src=t.getAttribute("data-src")),t.getAttribute("data-srcset")&&t.setAttribute("srcset",t.getAttribute("data-srcset"));var n=",";if(t.getAttribute("data-background-delimiter")&&(n=t.getAttribute("data-background-delimiter")),t.getAttribute("data-background-image"))t.style.backgroundImage="url('"+t.getAttribute("data-background-image").split(n).join("'),url('")+"')";else if(t.getAttribute("data-background-image-set")){var d=t.getAttribute("data-background-image-set").split(n),u=d[0].substr(0,d[0].indexOf(" "))||d[0];// Substring before ... 1x
u=-1===u.indexOf("url(")?"url("+u+")":u,1===d.length?t.style.backgroundImage=u:t.setAttribute("style",(t.getAttribute("style")||"")+"background-image: "+u+"; background-image: -webkit-image-set("+d+"); background-image: image-set("+d+")")}t.getAttribute("data-toggle-class")&&t.classList.toggle(t.getAttribute("data-toggle-class"))},loaded:function(){}};function A(t){t.setAttribute("data-loaded",!0)}var m=function(t){return"true"===t.getAttribute("data-loaded")},v=function(t){var e=1<arguments.length&&void 0!==arguments[1]?arguments[1]:document;return t instanceof Element?[t]:t instanceof NodeList?t:e.querySelectorAll(t)};return function(){var r,a,o=0<arguments.length&&void 0!==arguments[0]?arguments[0]:".lozad",t=1<arguments.length&&void 0!==arguments[1]?arguments[1]:{},e=Object.assign({},f,t),i=e.root,n=e.rootMargin,d=e.threshold,u=e.load,g=e.loaded,s=void 0;"undefined"!=typeof window&&window.IntersectionObserver&&(s=new IntersectionObserver((r=u,a=g,function(t,e){t.forEach(function(t){(0<t.intersectionRatio||t.isIntersecting)&&(e.unobserve(t.target),m(t.target)||(r(t.target),A(t.target),a(t.target)))})}),{root:i,rootMargin:n,threshold:d}));for(var c,l=v(o,i),b=0;b<l.length;b++)(c=l[b]).getAttribute("data-placeholder-background")&&(c.style.background=c.getAttribute("data-placeholder-background"));return{observe:function(){for(var t=v(o,i),e=0;e<t.length;e++)m(t[e])||(s?s.observe(t[e]):(u(t[e]),A(t[e]),g(t[e])))},triggerLoad:function(t){m(t)||(u(t),A(t),g(t))},observer:s}}});

</script>

<!--  Start: Scripts Migrated From Unit42-v5 -->
<script type="text/javascript">
	function getCookie(cname) {
	 	var name = cname + "=";
  		var decodedCookie = decodeURIComponent(document.cookie);
		var ca = decodedCookie.split(';');
  		for(var i = 0; i <ca.length; i++) {
    			var c = ca[i];
    			while (c.charAt(0) == ' ') {
     				 c = c.substring(1);
    			}
    			if (c.indexOf(name) == 0) {
    				 return c.substring(name.length, c.length);
    			}
  		}
  		return "";
	}

	var referer = "";//sessionStorage.container;
	var pcontainer = sessionStorage.getItem("container");
	var searchResultsPagePath = "";
	if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Prisma')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Prisma' ;
	}        
        else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Cortex')!=-1)){
            if( pcontainer.indexOf('CloudCortex') != -1){
                referer = 'CloudCortex' ;
            }
            else{
                referer = 'Cortex' ;
            }	    
	}
        else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Sase')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Sase' ;
	}
	else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Unit')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Unit' ;
	}
	else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Ngfw')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Ngfw' ;
	}
        var fromRef = document.referrer;
        var nContainer = getCookie("navContainer");
        if(nContainer){//If user is coming from main site, we need to reset the container		
		if(fromRef  && fromRef.indexOf("prismacloud.io")!=-1){
                        referer = 'Prisma' ;
                        sessionStorage.setItem("container","Prisma");
                } else if(fromRef.indexOf("paloaltonetworks.com")!=-1 || fromRef.indexOf("paloaltonetworks.jp")!=-1 ){
                        if(nContainer.indexOf('Prisma') != -1){
                            referer = 'Prisma' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Prisma");
                        }
                        if(nContainer.indexOf('Cortex') != -1){
                            if( nContainer.indexOf('CloudCortex') != -1){
                                referer = 'CloudCortex';
                                sessionStorage.setItem("container","CloudCortex");
                            }
                            else{
                                referer = 'Cortex';
                                sessionStorage.setItem("container","Cortex");
                            }                            
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Sase') != -1){
                            referer = 'Sase' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Sase");
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Unit') != -1){
                            referer = 'Unit' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Unit");
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Ngfw') != -1){
                            referer = 'Ngfw' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Ngfw");
                        }
			document.cookie = 'navContainer=; path=/; domain=.paloaltonetworks.com; expires=' + new Date(0).toUTCString();
		}
	}
if(referer != "Prisma" && referer != "CloudCortex" && referer != "Cortex" && referer != "Sase" && referer != "Unit" && referer != "Ngfw") {
    referer = 'Unit' ;
    sessionStorage.setItem("container","Unit");
}
function callMainSitePrismaNavHTML(){
    
    var referrer_domain = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
    sessionStorage.setItem("domain",referrer_domain);
    if(referer == 'Prisma'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderPrisma.prismaRenderer.html';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=prisma&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/prismasearch";
        }
    if(referer == 'CloudCortex'){
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=cortexcloud&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/cortexcloudsearch";	
    }
    if(referer == 'Cortex'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderCortex.cortexRenderer.html';	
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=cortex&domain=en';	
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/cortexsearch";	
    }
    if(referer == 'Sase'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderSase.saseRenderer.html';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=sase&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/sasesearch";
    }
    if(referer == 'Unit'){
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=unit42&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/content/pan/en_US/search/unit42search";
		    }
    if(referer == 'Ngfw'){
        //var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/ngfw-cdss-nav-renderer.php';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=ngfw&domain=en'; 
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/ngfwcdsssearch";
    }
    httpGet(menu_url,'menu_html');
    document.getElementById('main-nav-menu-cont').removeAttribute("style");
}
function addStyle(styles) {            
    /* Create style document */
    var css = document.createElement('style');
    css.type = 'text/css';

    if (css.styleSheet) 
        css.styleSheet.cssText = styles;
    else 
        css.appendChild(document.createTextNode(styles));

    /* Append style to the tag name */
    document.getElementsByTagName("head")[0].appendChild(css);
}
function httpGet(theUrl,req_type) {
    if (window.XMLHttpRequest) { // code for IE7+, Firefox, Chrome, Opera, Safari
        xmlhttp=new XMLHttpRequest();
    } else {// code for IE6, IE5
        xmlhttp=new ActiveXObject("Microsoft.XMLHTTP");
    }
    xmlhttp.onreadystatechange=function()
    {
        if (xmlhttp.readyState==4 && xmlhttp.status==200)
        {
            if(req_type == 'menu_html'){
            var nav_text = xmlhttp.responseText.replaceAll('https://static.cloud.coveo.com/searchui/v2.9159/js/CoveoJsSearch.Lazy.min.js', '');
            nav_text = nav_text.replaceAll('src="/', 'src="'+maindomain_lang+'/');
            nav_text = nav_text.replaceAll("'/content", "'"+maindomain_lang+"/content");
                        
            document.getElementById("PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC").innerHTML = nav_text.replaceAll('href="/', 'href="'+maindomain_lang+'/');

            var lozad_back = document.getElementsByClassName('lozad-background');
            Array.prototype.forEach.call(lozad_back, function(el) {
            // Do stuff here
            var el_back_img_path = el.getAttribute('data-background-image');
            var first_pos = el_back_img_path.indexOf("'");
            var last_pos = el_back_img_path.indexOf("'",first_pos+1);
            el_back_img_path = el_back_img_path.substring(first_pos+1,last_pos);
            let result = el_back_img_path.includes(main_site_url);
            if(result == false){
                el.setAttribute("data-background-image",main_site_url+el_back_img_path);
            }
            else{
                el.setAttribute("data-background-image",el_back_img_path);
            }
            });
            const observer_lozad = lozad('.lozad, .lozad-background'); // lazy loads elements with default selector as '.lozad'
            observer_lozad.observe();
            }
            if(req_type == 'head_inline_css'){
            addStyle(xmlhttp.responseText);
            }
        }
    }
    xmlhttp.open("GET", theUrl, true );
    xmlhttp.send();    
}    
    
if(referer == 'Prisma' || referer == 'CloudCortex' || referer == 'Cortex' || referer == 'Sase' || referer == 'Unit' || referer == 'Ngfw'){
    const article = document.querySelector('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC');
    if(referer == 'Prisma'){
        article.dataset.type = 'prisma';
        $('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC').removeClass('default').addClass('defaultRedesigned');
    }
    else if(referer == 'CloudCortex'){
        article.dataset.type = 'cloudcortex';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Cortex'){
        article.dataset.type = 'cortex';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Sase'){
        article.dataset.type = 'sase';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Unit'){
        article.dataset.type = 'unit';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Ngfw'){
        article.dataset.type = 'ngfw';
    }
    //set class to default
    if(referer == 'Unit' || referer == 'Ngfw'){
        $('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC').removeClass('default').addClass('defaultRedesigned');
    }
    callMainSitePrismaNavHTML();
}
</script>
<!--  End: Scripts Migrated From Unit42-v5 -->

alt="Advanced URL Filtering icon">Advanced URL Filtering</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/advanced-wildfire/" style="--card-color: #ffcb06" src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/strata_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Advanced WildFire icon">Advanced WildFire</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/cloud-delivered-security-services/" style="--card-color: #ffcb06" role="link" title="Cloud-Delivered Security Services" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Cloud-Delivered Security Services"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/strata_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Cloud-Delivered Security Services icon">Cloud-Delivered Security Services</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/cortex/" style="--card-color: #00cc66" role="link" title="Cortex" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Cortex"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/cortex_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Cortex icon">Cortex</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/cortex-xdr/" style="--card-color: #00cc66" role="link" title="Cortex XDR" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Cortex XDR"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/cortex_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Cortex XDR icon">Cortex XDR</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/cortex-xsiam/" style="--card-color: #00cc66" role="link" title="Cortex XSIAM" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Cortex XSIAM"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/cortex_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Cortex XSIAM icon">Cortex XSIAM</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/next-generation-firewall/" style="--card-color: #ffcb06" role="link" src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/strata_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Next-Generation Firewall icon">Next-Generation Firewall</a><a class="l-linkcard is-blue" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/product-category/next-generation-firewall-vm-series/" style="--card-color: #ffcb06" role="link" title="Next-Generation Firewall VM series" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Next-Generation Firewall VM series"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/strata_RGB_logo_Icon_Color.png" alt="Next-Generation Firewall VM series icon">Next-Generation Firewall VM series</a></div>              </div>
            </div>
          </div>
          <div class="ab__footer">
            <div class="l-container">
              <div class="ab__footer-wrapper">
                <ul class="ab__features" role="list">
                  <li role="listitem">
				 <img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/images/icons/icon-profile-grey.svg" alt="Profile Icon">
					 <div class="ab__text"><span>By:</span><ul class="ab__tags"><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Tom Fakterman" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/author/tom-fakterman/">Tom Fakterman</a></li></ul></div></li>                  <li role="listitem">
			<img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/images/icons/icon-calendar-grey.svg" alt="Published Icon">
			<div class="ab__text"><span>Published:</span>March 6, 2026</div></li>					<li role="listitem"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/images/icons/icon-category.svg" alt="Tags Icon"><div class="ab__text"><span>Categories:</span><ul class="ab__tags"><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Malware" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/category/malware/">Malware</a></li><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Threat Research" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/category/threat-research/">Threat Research</a></li></ul></div>
		</li>                  <li role="listitem"><img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/images/icons/icon-tags-grey.svg" alt="Tags Icon"><div class="ab__text"><span>Tags:</span><ul class="ab__tags"><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:China" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/china/">China</a></li><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:CL-UNK-1068" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/cl-unk-1068/">CL-UNK-1068</a></li><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:DLL Sideloading" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/dll-sideloading/">DLL Sideloading</a></li><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Fast Reverse Proxy" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/fast-reverse-proxy/">Fast Reverse Proxy</a></li><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:ScanPortPlus" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/scanportplus/">ScanPortPlus</a></li><li><a data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="cl-unk-1068-targets-critical-sectors:hero:Xnote" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/xnote/">Xnote</a></li></ul></div>
		</li>                </ul>
                <div class="ab__options">

            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </section> 
    <section class="section blog-contents"> 
      <div class="pa blog-editor">
        <div class="l-container">
          <div class="be__wrapper">
            <div class="be__contents">
              <div class="be__contents-wrapper">
                <h2><a id="post-174811-_4lt92rr5muov"></a>Executive Summary</h2>
<p>Since at least 2020, we have observed a cluster of activity targeting high-value organizations across South, Southeast and East Asia. The attacks focus on critical sectors such as aviation, energy, government, law enforcement, pharmaceutical, technology and telecommunications.</p>
<p>Unit 42 is tracking this ongoing, previously undocumented activity as CL-UNK-1068. We designate the term UNK to clusters of activity whose affiliation with either nation-state or cybercrime activity we have not yet determined.</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">We assess with high confidence that the attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 are a Chinese threat actor. This assessment is based on the origin of their tools, linguistic artifacts in configuration files, and their consistent, longstanding targeting of critical infrastructure in Asia. We assess with moderate-to-high confidence that the primary objective of the attackers is cyberespionage, although we cannot fully rule out the possibility of cybercriminal motivation at this time.</span></p>
<p>Through a long period of close observation, we identified the specific tools and techniques that define this group. Our attribution of this activity to CL-UNK-1068 is done in accordance with <a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/unit-42-attribution-framework/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42’s attribution framework</a>. We provide a detailed analysis of the attack patterns and methods that we identified in our investigation into this cluster of activity.</p>
<p>Palo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats described through the following products and services:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/advanced-url-filtering/administration" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced URL Filtering</a> and <a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/dns-security" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced DNS Security</a></li>
<li><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/network-security/next-generation-firewall" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Next-Generation Firewall</a> (NGFW) with <a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/network-security/advanced-threat-prevention" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced Threat Prevention</a></li>
<li><a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/wildfire" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced WildFire</a></li>
<li><a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/p/XDR" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex XDR</a> and <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/p/XSIAM" target="_blank" rel="noopener">XSIAM</a></li>
</ul>
<p>If you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, contact the <a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Incident Response team</a>.</p>
<table style="width: 100%; height: 24px;">
<tbody>
<tr style="height: 24px;">
<td style="width: 35%; height: 24px;"><b>Related Unit 42 Topics</b></td>
<td style="width: 100%; height: 24px;"><b><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/china/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">China</a>, CL-UNK-1068, <a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/dll-sideloading/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">DLL Sideloading</a>, <a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/backdoor/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Backdoors</a></b></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<h2><a id="post-174811-_wven14kmgum2"></a>Technical Analysis Overview</h2>
<p>We provide a detailed analysis of the tool set deployed by the attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 across different intrusion campaigns since 2020. While these attacks demonstrate a consistent set of techniques and procedures (TTPs), it is important to note that not every tool was used in every observed intrusion. Our analysis reveals a multi-faceted tool set that includes custom malware, modified open-source utilities and living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBINs). These provide a simple, effective way for the attackers to maintain a persistent presence within targeted environments.</p>
<p>The CL-UNK-1068 activity cluster is characterized by cross-platform cyber capabilities, maintaining a diverse set of tools for both Windows and Linux environments. Their TTPs rely heavily on open-source utilities and malware variants popular with Chinese-speaking users, including <a href="https://github.com/BeichenDream/Godzilla/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">GodZilla</a>, <a href="https://github.com/AntSwordProject" target="_blank" rel="noopener">AntSword</a>, <a href="https://github.com/fatedier/frp" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Xnote</a> and <a href="https://github.com/fatedier/frp" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Fast Reverse Proxy</a> (FRP). One of the techniques we observed in these attacks is the use of legitimate Python executables to launch DLL side-loading attacks. This approach enables the attackers to stealthily load additional payloads.</p>
<h2><a id="post-174811-_xbsx8wbqpi30"></a>Initial Access and Web Shell Deployment</h2>
<p>The initial access to environments targeted in CL-UNK-1068 activity is achieved by deploying and utilizing various web shells. We observed the attackers deploying the GodZilla web shell, and a variation of AntSword, both of which are written in a combination of English and Simplified Chinese. After gaining an initial foothold, the attackers use these web shells to move laterally to additional hosts and SQL servers. Figure 1 shows an alert that was triggered when an attacker attempted to exploit a Linux server.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174812" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174812" style="width: 900px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174812 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-994086-174811-1.png" alt="A screenshot of a notification card with a red &quot;H&quot; icon. The title reads &quot;Webserver Exploitation,&quot; and the source is marked as &quot;XDR BIOC&quot; with a warning icon. Below, it says &quot;nginx process spawning a remote shell.&quot; There are small icons on the upper right side." width="900" height="291" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-994086-174811-1.png 1223w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-994086-174811-1-786x254.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-994086-174811-1-768x248.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 900px) 100vw, 900px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174812" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 1. Cortex XDR alert indicating Linux webserver exploitation, triggered by CL-UNK-1068 activity.</figcaption></figure>
<h2><a id="post-174811-_yveul590xmbt"></a>Exfiltrating Configuration Files for Access and Sensitive Data</h2>
<p>After gaining access to targeted environments, the attackers attempt to steal the following files from the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">c:\inetpub\wwwroot</span> directory of a Windows web server:</p>
<ul>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">web.config</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.aspx</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.asmx</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.asax</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.dll</span></li>
</ul>
<p>The attackers could use this stolen information to extract credentials for lateral movement, or to discover vulnerabilities in the website's code.</p>
<p>The alert in Figure 2 shows that the attackers archived the stolen files under the names <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">web.rar, web1.rar</span> and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">web2.rar</span>.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174823" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174823" style="width: 900px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174823 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-996268-174811-2.png" alt="A flowchart in Cortex XDR displaying a sequence of processes involving two executable files triggering multiple instances of of a EXE file labeled RAR. Each &quot;rar.exe&quot; process shows a command line path involving compression of files within the C drive. Multiple icons indicate the malware alert process. " width="900" height="642" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-996268-174811-2.png 1031w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-996268-174811-2-617x440.png 617w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-996268-174811-2-982x700.png 982w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-996268-174811-2-768x548.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 900px) 100vw, 900px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174823" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 2. Cortex XDR alert showing the attackers archiving files for exfiltration under <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">c:\inetpub\wwwroot</span>.</figcaption></figure>
<p>After moving to additional servers, the attackers continued to steal files related to the website’s configuration, such as <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.json</span> files from the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">c:\inetpub\wwwroot</span> directory, including the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">appsettings.json</span> file.</p>
<p>In multiple instances, the attackers used a simple but effective approach to exfiltrate files:</p>
<ol>
<li>Using WinRAR to archive the relevant files.</li>
<li>Executing the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">certutil -encode</span> command to Base64-encode the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.rar</span> archives.</li>
<li>Executing the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">type</span> command to print the Base64 content to their screen through the web shell.</li>
</ol>
<p>By encoding the archives as text and printing them to their screen, the attackers were able to exfiltrate data without actually uploading any files. The attackers likely chose this method because the shell on the host allowed them to run commands and view output, but not to directly transfer files. Figure 3 shows the alert triggered by the data exfiltration activity.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174834" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174834" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174834 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-998731-174811-3.png" alt="A flowchart in Cortex XDR displaying the CL-UNK-1068 data exfiltration process, showing the sequence of file archiving with WinRAR, Base64 encoding, and outputting the data via a command-line tool for web shell retrieval." width="1000" height="563" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-998731-174811-3.png 1304w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-998731-174811-3-782x440.png 782w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-998731-174811-3-1244x700.png 1244w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-998731-174811-3-768x432.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174834" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 3. Cortex XDR alert showing the attackers exfiltrating archived files.</figcaption></figure>
<p>In addition to stealing configuration files, the attackers stole other types of sensitive data:</p>
<ul>
<li>Browser history and web browser bookmarks</li>
<li>Sensitive <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">XLSX</span> and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">CSV</span> files from desktops and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">USER</span> directories</li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.bak</span> files from MSSQL servers (database backup files)</li>
</ul>
<p>In certain instances, the attackers deployed <a href="https://github.com/xo/usql" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">usql</span></a>, a universal command-line interface for multiple databases. The use of this interface may indicate that one of the goals of CL-UNK-1068 activity is to extract data directly from SQL servers.</p>
<h2><a id="post-174811-_hejndlixrd70"></a>Tool Set</h2>
<p>We analyzed the most noteworthy tools and utilities that the attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 used across multiple intrusion campaigns since 2020. A detailed analysis of additional tools and utilities used during this activity is provided in <a href="#post-174811-_dqn7va5cjyh6" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Appendix B</a>.</p>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_yz6u5qyx6ntr"></a>DLL Side-Loading Using Legacy Python Programming Language Executables</h3>
<p>In attacks that we observed, the attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 frequently used DLL side-loading to execute their tool set. They deployed a legitimate Python programming language executable like <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python.exe</span> or <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">pythonw.exe</span> alongside a malicious side-loaded DLL that served as a loader, using a name like <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python20.dll</span>. The attackers also dropped an obfuscated shellcode file with a similar name, to match the legitimate executable naming convention (e.g., <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python</span> or <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">pythonw</span>).</p>
<p>When the legitimate <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python.exe</span> is executed, it side-loads a malicious loader named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python20.dll</span>. The malicious loader reads the obfuscated shellcode, deobfuscates it in memory, and then executes it within the memory space of the legitimate Python process. The shellcode then decrypts and executes the payload in memory.</p>
<p>The attackers used this technique to load and execute several tools as payloads, including <a href="https://github.com/fatedier/frp" target="_blank" rel="noopener">FRP</a>, <a href="https://github.com/itm4n/PrintSpoofer" target="_blank" rel="noopener">PrintSpoofer</a> and a custom scanner that they named ScanPortPlus. Figure 4 shows the legitimate <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python.exe</span> process used to read shellcode from a file named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python</span> and execute a decrypted payload for ScanPortPlus in memory.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174845" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174845" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174845 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-1034-174811-4.png" alt="A flowchart in Cortex XDR alert showing a legitimate Python executable is used for DLL side-loading to execute the decrypted ScanPortPlus payload in memory." width="1000" height="418" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-1034-174811-4.png 1512w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-1034-174811-4-786x329.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-1034-174811-4-768x321.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174845" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 4. Cortex XDR alert showing that <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python.exe</span> reads shellcode from the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">python</span> file and executes the decrypted ScanPortPlus in memory.</figcaption></figure>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_8ja0paqekmk"></a>ScanPortPlus: A Custom Multi-Platform Scanning Toolkit</h3>
<p>The attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 scanned compromised networks using a custom scanner that they internally named ScanPortPlus. This custom tool is written in <a href="https://go.dev/learn/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Go</a>, and the threat actor compiled versions for both Windows and Linux systems. Figure 5 shows the command-line options of ScanPortPlus, which include IP address, port and vulnerability scanning.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174856" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174856" style="width: 800px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174856 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-3140-174811-5.png" alt="A screenshot of command-line options for the ScanPortPlus custom scanning toolkit, detailing functions for IP address, port, and vulnerability scanning." width="800" height="465" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174856" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 5. ScanPortPlus command-line options.</figcaption></figure>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_t3r8auosme51"></a>Communication Tunneling: Custom FRP Variant with Unique Identifiers</h3>
<p>In some of the events that we observed, the attackers deployed <a href="https://github.com/fatedier/frp" target="_blank" rel="noopener">FRP</a>, to establish persistent access while bypassing firewalls. The attackers used versions of their own custom-compiled FRP for Windows and Linux systems, including a custom FRP that had several unique identifiers:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Unique authentication token: </strong>Attackers used the authentication token <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">frpforzhangwei</span> (“frp for zhang wei”). Zhang Wei is a common Chinese name.</li>
<li><strong>Proxy naming convention:</strong> The proxy names appear to have a consistent naming convention across the versions:
<ul>
<li>Windows: <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">10014-win-nic-32-v</span></li>
<li>Linux:
<ul>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">20012-linux-64-V</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">10013-linux-64-V</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Unique common password</strong>: The password for the FRP is the same in all samples that the threat actor used: <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">f*ckroot123</span> (profanity masked).</li>
</ul>
<p>Figure 6 highlights the identifiers that we discovered in the FRP samples.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174867" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174867" style="width: 900px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174867 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-4936-174811-6.png" alt="A sceenshot of a configuration from the custom Fast Reverse Proxy (FRP) samples, including the unique authentication token and consistent proxy naming conventions used by CL-UNK-1068." width="900" height="688" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-4936-174811-6.png 938w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-4936-174811-6-576x440.png 576w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-4936-174811-6-916x700.png 916w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-4936-174811-6-768x587.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 900px) 100vw, 900px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174867" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 6. Configuration from FRP samples used in CL-UNK-1068 activity.</figcaption></figure>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_xr7c9ytp6nez"></a>Deploying Xnote Linux Backdoor</h3>
<p>In some instances, the attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 deployed the Xnote malware on Linux servers. First discovered in 2015, <a href="https://vms.drweb.com/virus/?i=4372602" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Xnote</a> is a Linux backdoor that various Chinese threat actors <a href="https://www.trendmicro.com/vinfo/us/security/news/cybercrime-and-digital-threats/exposing-earth-berberoka-a-multiplatform-apt-campaign-targeting-online-gambling-sites" target="_blank" rel="noopener">previously used</a>. Xnote has several variants, each with slightly different functionality. The Xnote used by CL-UNK-1068 primarily provides distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack capabilities, in addition to other commands.</p>
<p>Table 1 lists some of the capabilities of this Xnote variant.</p>
<table style="width: 100.782%;">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%; text-align: center;"><strong>Internal Task Name</strong></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%; text-align: center;"><strong>Task Function</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">9CFileTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Interact with file system, upload and download files, execute shell commands</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">10CShellTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Reverse shell</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">10CProxyTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">No current function; likely a remnant from previous versions, since replaced by 13CNewProxyTask</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">11CDDosCCTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Execute a <a href="https://www.tencentcloud.com/techpedia/117843" target="_blank" rel="noopener">CC DDoS attack</a></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">12CDdosNtpTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Execute <a href="https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/ddos/ntp-amplification-ddos-attack/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">NTP DDoS attack</a></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">12CDDosSynTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Execute <a href="https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/ddos/syn-flood-ddos-attack/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">SYN Flood DDoS attack</a></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">12CDDosUDPTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Execute a <a href="https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/ddos/udp-flood-ddos-attack/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">UDP Flood DDoS attack</a></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">12CPortMapTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Establish port forwarding on tde machine</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="width: 22.8867%;"><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">13CNewProxyTask</span></td>
<td style="width: 97.0447%;">Set a reverse proxy or tunnel</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p><span style="font-size: 10pt;">Table 1. Xnote task names and functions.</span></p>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_8wrblqlktgkc"></a>Host-Level Reconnaissance Operations</h3>
<p>Our observations reveal that in 2020, the attackers deployed a custom tool named SuperDump for reconnaissance. In the years following, we saw that the attackers transitioned to a new method of using batch scripts for reconnaissance purposes.</p>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_1m8hh1luwoso"></a>Gathering Host Information Using Custom SuperDump Tool</h4>
<p>In intrusions dating back to 2020, the attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 attempted to use a custom .NET tool that they named SuperDump. The tool’s purpose is to collect information from Windows hosts, such as:</p>
<ul>
<li>User information</li>
<li>Host information: IP address, running processes, system information, drive information</li>
<li>Files from desktop and document folders</li>
<li>Installed programs</li>
<li>Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) process dump content</li>
<li>Registry information:
<ul>
<li>Navicat configuration (database management tool)</li>
<li>WinSCP configuration</li>
<li>RDP configuration</li>
<li>Internet Explorer settings</li>
<li>Environment variables</li>
<li>PuTTY configuration</li>
<li>FileZila data</li>
<li>NetSarang Xmanager data (remote desktop software)</li>
<li>SSH data</li>
<li>PowerShell history</li>
<li>Microsoft\Windows\Recent registry key (recent programs)</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<p>Figure 7 shows the functions in SuperDump’s code that gather information.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174878" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174878" style="width: 600px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174878 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-7525-174811-7.png" alt="A code snippet in C# language. Function names from the SuperDump tool's code responsible for gathering extensive host reconnaissance information on Windows systems." width="600" height="551" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-7525-174811-7.png 482w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-7525-174811-7-479x440.png 479w" sizes="(max-width: 600px) 100vw, 600px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174878" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 7. Function names in SuperDump code that are responsible for information gathering.</figcaption></figure>
<p>We discovered that the use of SuperDump was later replaced by batch script files called <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">hpp.bat</span> and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">hp.bat,</span> which also collect host information. The functionality of these batch files is detailed in the following section.</p>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_1ulldt6173pl"></a>Host Reconnaissance Using Batch Scripts</h4>
<p>In more recently observed cases, after successfully compromising an endpoint, the attackers initiate the reconnaissance phase. This involves deploying custom batch scripts to gather initial host telemetry and map the local environment.</p>
<p>The specific naming conventions for both scripts and output files constitute a unique signature that we observed across multiple attacks over several years.</p>
<p>We observed that in several instances, the attackers executed a batch script named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">hp.bat</span> or <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">hpp.bat</span>, and on one occasion, <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">a.bat</span>. Each of these batch scripts executed multiple commands and saved the results in matching <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">.txt</span> files. The attackers utilized these scripts to perform host reconnaissance, gather telemetry on the local system and map other potential servers in the environment. For a detailed analysis of the scripts, output filenames and executed commands, see <a href="#post-174811-_dqn7va5cjyh6" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Appendix B</a>.</p>
<p>After all the output files were written to disk, attackers executed an additional <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">rar.bat/rr.bat</span> batch script that was responsible for archiving the result files using commands such as:</p>
<ul>
<li>​​<span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">rar.exe a -df host.rar *.txt</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">rar a -df host.rar *.txt *.db</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">rar a -df host.rar *.txt *.db *hist* *book*</span></li>
</ul>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_1r68zpx392aa"></a>Credential Theft Tool Set</h3>
<p>This section provides a comprehensive description of the various tools and methods utilized in CL-UNK-1068 activity to execute credential theft.</p>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_wgm9j8tekcio"></a>Mimikatz and LsaRecorder</h4>
<p>The attackers used Mimikatz to dump passwords from memory, and a dumping tool named LsaRecorder, as Figure 8 shows.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174889" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174889" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174889 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-9482-174811-8.png" alt="A flowchart showing execution view of the LsaRecorder tool, a memory-dumping utility used by the threat actor for password theft." width="1000" height="235" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-9482-174811-8.png 963w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-9482-174811-8-786x184.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-9482-174811-8-768x180.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174889" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 8. Execution of LsaRecorder.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The LsaRecorder tool captures login passwords by hooking the <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/ntsecpkg/nc-ntsecpkg-lsa_ap_logon_user_ex2" target="_blank" rel="noopener">LsaApLogonUserEx2</a> callback function. The <a href="https://bbs.kanxue.com/thread-251888.htm" target="_blank" rel="noopener">LsaRecorder tool</a> was shared on the Chinese security forum called Kanxue in 2019. Figure 9 shows the LsaRecorder command-line options, which include the ability to record a user’s logon password.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174900" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174900" style="width: 700px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174900 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-11325-174811-9.png" alt="A code snippet showing a command line interface options for the LsaRecorder tool, highlighting its capability to capture a user’s logon password." width="700" height="120" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174900" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 9. Command-line options of LsaRecorder.</figcaption></figure>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_ffam9r9w7sbv"></a>DumpIt and Volatility</h4>
<p>The attackers behind CL-UNK-1068 attempted to use <a href="https://github.com/MagnetForensics/dumpit-linux" target="_blank" rel="noopener">DumpIt</a>, a free multiplatform forensics tool, in combination with the widely known <a href="https://github.com/volatilityfoundation/volatility" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Volatility</a> framework to extract password hashes from memory. As shown in Figure 10, they used DumpIt to dump the victim machine's memory. Next, they used several Volatility modules:</p>
<ul>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">windows.hashdump</span>: Extracts local user account NTLM password hashes from the SAM registry hive</li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">windows.registry.lsadump.Lsadump</span>: Dumps LSA Secrets such as service account passwords, cached domain credentials</li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">windows.registry.cachedump.Cachedump</span>: Dumps cached domain credentials</li>
</ul>
<p>In addition, in some instances the attackers executed DumpIt and Volatility, using batch scripts named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">dmp.bat</span> and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">vo.bat</span>.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174911" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174911" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174911 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-13194-174811-10.png" alt="A flowchart of Cortex XDR alert showing the combined use of the DumpIt and Volatility frameworks to extract password hashes from a compromised machine's memory." width="1000" height="405" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-13194-174811-10.png 1116w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-13194-174811-10-786x318.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-13194-174811-10-768x311.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174911" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 10. Cortex XDR alert triggered when attackers used DumpIt and Volatility to dump machine memory and extract password hashes.</figcaption></figure>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_nmpg2manb0o9"></a>SQL Server Management Studio Password Export Tool</h4>
<p>The <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">sqlstudio.bin</span> file stores saved connection info for Microsoft SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS). Attackers attempted to extract data from this file using a tool named SQL Server Management Studio Password Export Tool, deployed as <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">ssms.exe</span>. This <a href="https://www.zcgonvh.com/post/SQL_Server_Management_Studio_saved_password_dumper.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">tool was published</a> on a Chinese security blog in 2015.</p>
<p>The attackers ran the tool locally and attempted to exfiltrate the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">sqlstudio.bin</span> file. They used the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">certutil -encode</span> command to Base64-encode the file, and the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">type</span> command to read the encoded file. Figure 11 shows this sequence of events.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174922" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174922" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174922 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-15321-174811-11.png" alt="A flowchart of Cortex XDR alert showing the use of a tool to extract saved passwords from SQL Server Management Studio configuration files." width="1000" height="403" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-15321-174811-11.png 1088w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-15321-174811-11-786x317.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-15321-174811-11-768x310.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174922" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 11. Cortex XDR alert triggered when attackers used the SQL Server Management Studio Password Export Tool to extract passwords.</figcaption></figure>
<h2><a id="post-174811-_2an8ryq91inv"></a>Conclusion</h2>
<p>We assess with high confidence that CL-UNK-1068 represents activity from a threat group that communicates in Chinese. The group behind this activity cluster has been targeting high-value sectors across South, Southeast and East Asia since at least 2020. Using primarily open-source tools, community-shared malware and batch scripts, the group has successfully maintained stealthy operations while infiltrating critical organizations.</p>
<p>This cluster of activity demonstrates versatility by operating across both Windows and Linux environments, using different versions of their tool set for each operating system. While the focus on credential theft and sensitive data exfiltration from critical infrastructure and government sectors strongly suggests an espionage motive, we cannot yet fully rule out cybercriminal intentions.</p>
<p>We advise defenders to move beyond static indicators and focus on behavioral anomalies. Detection logic should be tuned to identify any hallmark techniques. In the case of CL-UNK-1068 activity, signs to detect include:</p>
<ul>
<li>Misuse of legitimate Python binaries for side-loading</li>
<li>Deployment of unauthorized tunneling tools like FRP</li>
<li>Execution of custom reconnaissance batch scripts</li>
</ul>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_lsbythhc3nz9"></a>Palo Alto Networks Protection and Mitigation</h3>
<p>Palo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed above through the following products and services:</p>
<h3><a id="post-174811-_c3iyw6lkrwlp"></a>Cortex Xpanse</h3>
<p><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cortex-xpanse" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex Xpanse</a> has the ability to identify exposed VMWare vCenter Server devices on the public internet and escalate these findings to defenders. Customers can enable alerting on this risk by ensuring that they’ve enabled the VMware vCenter Attack Surface Rule. Identified findings can be viewed in the incident view of Expander. These findings are also available for Cortex XSIAM customers who have purchased the ASM module.</p>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_9iwtl6yifwu9"></a>Cortex XDR Forensics: Linux</h4>
<p>The <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-XDR/Cortex-XDR-3.x-Documentation/Forensic-investigations" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Forensics</a> feature of Cortex XDR enables analysts to perform forensic analysis by collecting all necessary artifacts and displaying them in an intuitive forensics console. This feature also enables in-depth analysis of specific endpoints, to fully understand the activities that occurred. Supported forensic artifacts include environment variables, command history, session history, network connections and file listing. Figure 12 shows the command history of a CL-UNK-1068 interactive attack on a Linux server.</p>
<figure id="attachment_174933" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174933" style="width: 900px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174933 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-17324-174811-12.png" alt="A command history on a Linux server, captured by Cortex XDR Forensics, detailing an interactive attack by the CL-UNK-1068 threat actor." width="900" height="847" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-17324-174811-12.png 1118w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-17324-174811-12-468x440.png 468w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-17324-174811-12-744x700.png 744w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-17324-174811-12-768x723.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 900px) 100vw, 900px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174933" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 12. Command history of a Linux server during a CL-UNK-1068 interactive attack.</figcaption></figure>
<h4><a id="post-174811-_f9f5h3at1aqr"></a>Cortex XDR Analytics: Linux</h4>
<p>The new Cortex XDR <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-XDR/Cortex-XDR-3.x-Documentation/Analytics-engine" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Analytics Engine</a> enhances behavioral detection for Linux through two key mechanisms:</p>
<ul>
<li>Uncommon Linux process communication to a rare external host: This detector flags command-and-control (C2) initiation. Tailored for Linux, it identifies low-prevalence or recurring outbound patterns that are used by advanced threats to maintain network connections.</li>
<li>Uncommon attempt to discover a sensitive file: This detector identifies credential theft attempts, such as unauthorized access to <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">/etc/hosts</span> and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">/etc/ssl/private/.*</span>. This exposes misused utilities and threat actor activity targeting user secrets. Figure 13 displays an “Uncommon attempt” alert that CL-UNK-1068 activity triggered.</li>
</ul>
<figure id="attachment_174944" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-174944" style="width: 700px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-174944 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/word-image-19529-174811-13.png" alt="A screenshot of a Cortex XDR security alert flagging an uncommon attempt by the FRP tunneling tool to access sensitive files." width="700" height="261" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-174944" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 13. Cortex XDR alert for FRP attempting to access /etc/hosts during a CL-UNK-1068 attack.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Other Palo Alto Networks products and services that can help include:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/advanced-url-filtering/administration" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced URL Filtering</a> and <a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/dns-security" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced DNS Security</a> identify known domains and URLs associated with this activity as malicious.</li>
<li><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/network-security/next-generation-firewall" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Next-Generation Firewall</a> with the <a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/network-security/advanced-threat-prevention">Advanced Threat Prevention</a> security subscription can help block the attacks with best practices via the following Threat Prevention signature/s 94655, 91671, 91662, 86680, 81881, 81819, 81815, 81816, 81817, 81803</li>
<li>The <a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/wildfire" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced WildFire</a> machine-learning models and analysis techniques have been reviewed and updated in light of the indicators shared in this research.</li>
<li><a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/p/XSIAM" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex XSIAM</a> incorporates all <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/p/XDR" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex XDR</a> features, as well as additional protections.</li>
</ul>
<p>If you think you may have been compromised or have an urgent matter, get in touch with the <a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Incident Response team</a> or call:</p>
<ul>
<li>North America: Toll Free: +1 (866) 486-4842 (866.4.UNIT42)</li>
<li>UK: +44.20.3743.3660</li>
<li>Europe and Middle East: +31.20.299.3130</li>
<li>Asia: +65.6983.8730</li>
<li>Japan: +81.50.1790.0200</li>
<li>Australia: +61.2.4062.7950</li>
<li>India: 000 800 050 45107</li>
</ul>
<p>Palo Alto Networks has shared these findings with our fellow Cyber Threat Alliance (CTA) members. CTA members use this intelligence to rapidly deploy protections to their customers and to systematically disrupt malicious cyber actors. Learn more about the <a href="https://www.cyberthreatalliance.org" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cyber Threat Alliance</a>.</p>
<h2><a id="post-174811-_v8176g40kstn"></a>Indicators of Compromise</h2>

<ul>
